hero.tagline: Oregon's small claims courts offer a streamlined process for resolving disputes up to $10,000, with mandatory mediation in many counties.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $10,000, you cannot split it into multiple small claims to stay within the limit. Instead, you must file in the regular civil court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Oregon allows you to file small claims for breaches of contract and money owed, provided the amount does not exceed $10,000.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $2,000, and they failed to repay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement and any partial payments.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent a friend $5,000 with the understanding they'd repay in a year, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Document the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: Someone signed a promissory note to repay $3,000 but defaulted.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of default.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services
claims.example: You provided $1,500 worth of services, invoiced the client, but haven't been paid.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Keep copies of the invoice and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,000 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Maintain detailed account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,500 in back rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $1,000 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 30-day notice required before filing. Keep the returned check and any related correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $35 + civil penalty up to $500 or triple the check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,000 loan, the primary borrower defaulted, and you had to pay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Show the co-signed agreement and proof of your payments.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Oregon's small claims courts handle various consumer disputes, including defective products and false advertising, with a $10,000 limit.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $2,000 appliance that stopped working shortly after purchase, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Provide purchase receipts and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service
claims.example: A service provider charged $1,500 for work that was incomplete or poorly done.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Document the service agreement and evidence of poor workmanship.

claims.name: Dispute over auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $3,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Keep all repair invoices and records of subsequent issues.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $5,000 to remodel your bathroom, but they left the job unfinished.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods
claims.example: You paid $1,200 for furniture that was never delivered.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Keep the purchase agreement and any correspondence with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A company continued to charge your credit card $50 monthly after you canceled the subscription.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Document cancellation requests and unauthorized charges.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $7,000 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Provide the bill of sale and any repair estimates.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $500 with features it didn't have, leading you to purchase under false pretenses.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Keep copies of the advertisement and proof of purchase.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Oregon's small claims courts address property damage claims up to $10,000, including vehicle collisions and damage to rental properties.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $4,000 in damage to your car and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from incident. Provide police reports and repair estimates.

claims.name: Neighbor damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell on your fence, causing $1,500 in damage.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from incident. Document the damage with photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left $3,000 worth of damage beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery. Provide the lease agreement and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog dug up your garden, causing $800 in damage.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from incident. Document the damage with photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left a $2,000 bicycle with a friend, and it was damaged while in their possession.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery. Provide evidence of the agreement and photos of the damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Oregon's small claims courts handle monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $10,000, excluding eviction proceedings.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and move-out inspection reports.

claims.name: Landlord failed to provide itemized deductions
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Request the itemized list in writing before filing.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had severe mold issues, and the landlord refused to address them.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Document the conditions with photos and maintenance requests.

claims.name: Landlord retained tenant's personal property
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord kept your furniture valued at $2,000.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Provide evidence of ownership and communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your belongings.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from incident. Document the eviction with photos and witness statements.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but moved out early and the landlord refused to refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and proof of payment.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,500 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You repaired a broken heater for $600 and deducted it from rent, but the landlord disputes the deduction.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from repair. Keep receipts and communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Oregon's small claims courts handle wage disputes up to $10,000, including unpaid wages and final paychecks.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $3,000 in unpaid wages for hours worked.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Provide pay stubs and records of hours worked.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After leaving your job, your employer refused to issue your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Document your termination date and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Unpaid sales commissions
claims.example: Your employer owes you $4,000 in earned commissions but hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Provide the commission agreement and sales records.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime
claims.example: You worked 20 hours of overtime but weren't compensated at the correct rate.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Keep records of hours worked and pay received.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Oregon's small claims courts address personal claims up to $10,000, including minor injuries and civil theft.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from injury. Provide medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Damage to loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,500 camera, and they returned it broken.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery. Document the loan agreement and damage.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: Someone stole your bicycle worth $800, and you have evidence of their involvement.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery. Provide police reports and evidence of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Oregon's small claims courts handle vehicle-related disputes up to $10,000, including mechanic disputes and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the problem.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Keep all repair invoices and records of subsequent issues.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you incurred $300 in fees.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from incident. Provide photos of the parking area and towing receipts.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims courts do not handle eviction proceedings; these are addressed in the regular civil court.
Disputes involving the ownership of real property are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims courts.
Claims involving defamation, such as libel or slander, are not suitable for small claims court due to their complexity.
Professional malpractice claims require expert testimony and are not handled in small claims court.
Issues like divorce, child custody, and support are outside the scope of small claims court.
Disputes over wills and estates are handled by probate courts, not small claims courts.
Small claims courts do not have jurisdiction over cases involving federal entities.
Class action lawsuits are complex and not suitable for the simplified procedures of small claims court.
Small claims courts cannot issue orders requiring someone to do or stop doing something.
Disputes over workers' compensation are handled by specialized boards, not small claims courts.
Bankruptcy cases are under federal jurisdiction and not handled by state small claims courts.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are typically not eligible for small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Oregon small claims courts do not award punitive damages; however, statutory penalties may be available in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $35 + civil penalty up to $500 or triple the check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in certain cases, such as those involving bad checks or specific statutory violations.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Department of the Circuit Court

filing_options: Any county where ONE defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability varies by county; check with the local court for specific procedures.

service_fees.notes: Contact the local sheriff's office for current rates.
Fees depend on the server's rates.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver may cover filing fees and service costs.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: Oregon requires a bona fide effort to collect the claim before filing, typically through a demand letter.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: A 30-day written notice is required before filing a claim for a dishonored check.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: A written demand for unpaid wages is recommended before filing a claim.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of deductions from the security deposit within 31 days of lease termination.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: A written demand is recommended before filing under consumer protection statutes.

forms.name: Small Claims Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim against the defendant.

forms.name: Notice of Small Claim
forms.description: Notifies the defendant of the claim and the requirement to respond.

forms.name: Defendant's Response
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the claim and request a hearing.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the plaintiff to indicate that the judgment has been paid in full.

forms.name: Fee Deferral and Waiver Application
forms.description: Request to defer or waive court fees based on financial hardship.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: Court clerk sends the documents via certified mail to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A professional process server delivers the documents to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Oregon rules and the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court permission for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant must file a written response within 14 days of service to avoid default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to respond, the plaintiff can request a default judgment for the claimed amount.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even in default, the plaintiff may need to provide evidence to support the claim amount.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion to vacate must be filed within 30 days of the default judgment; if denied, an appeal can be filed within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds $10,000, the case may be transferred to the regular civil docket.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Attorneys are not allowed to represent parties in small claims hearings without the judge's permission.
hearing.format: Informal bench trial, typically lasting 30 minutes to an hour.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court as soon as possible before the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Many counties require mediation before proceeding to trial.

appeals.type_notes: Small claims judgments are final and not subject to appeal.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 9% per annum on unpaid judgments.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: 75% of disposable earnings or 40 times the federal minimum hourly wage per week, whichever is greater.

methods.name: Bank account levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of garnishment, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank freezes and turns over funds to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account; some funds may be exempt.
exemptions: $2,500 in a single account is exempt from garnishment.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: Record the judgment with the county recorder's office to create a lien on the debtor's real property, which must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property; may take time to realize payment.
exemptions: Homestead exemption up to $40,000 for individuals, $50,000 for joint owners.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, have the sheriff seize the debtor's non-exempt personal property, and sell it at auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.
exemptions: Household goods up to $3,000, vehicle up to $3,000, tools of trade up to $5,000.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 10-year period expires; a fee applies.
exempt_assets: Homestead — $40,000 for individuals, $50,000 for joint owners
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order of filing or recording the judgment.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney in small claims court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under an assumed name must register with the Oregon Secretary of State.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors can send a designated employee with written authorization.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face limitations in enforcing contracts in court.

county_variations.detail: Multnomah County requires mediation before proceeding to trial in small claims cases.
Coos County offers mediation in person, by phone, or by video for small claims cases.
Douglas County schedules mediation orientation for all small claims cases.

pitfalls.title: Failing to attempt pre-filing resolution
pitfalls.what_happens: The court may dismiss your case if you haven't made a bona fide effort to resolve the dispute before filing.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Send a detailed demand letter and keep records of all communication attempts.

pitfalls.title: Incorrectly naming the defendant
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party can result in dismissal or an unenforceable judgment.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the legal name and status of the defendant, especially for businesses, through the Oregon Secretary of State.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the deadline results in automatic dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Determine the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within that period.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Improper service can delay your case or lead to dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Oregon's rules for serving documents, using authorized methods and ensuring proof of service is filed.

pitfalls.title: Not preparing sufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can result in losing your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements to support your claim.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring post-judgment procedures
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; you must take steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods like garnishment and liens, and act promptly to enforce the judgment.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims filing fees
recent_changes.description: Filing fees for small claims cases were increased to $57 for claims up to $2,500 and $102 for claims over $2,500.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, attorneys are not allowed to represent parties in Oregon small claims court without the judge's permission. The process is designed for individuals to represent themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The typical timeline for a small claims case in Oregon is between 30 to 90 days from filing to resolution, depending on the court's schedule and whether mediation is required.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: In Oregon, the maximum amount you can sue for in small claims court is $10,000, excluding court costs and interest.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to respond or appear in court, you can request a default judgment for the amount claimed, plus any applicable fees and costs.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: No, small claims judgments in Oregon are final and not subject to appeal. It's important to present your best case during the initial hearing.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Oregon is valid for 10 years and can be renewed before it expires to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Oregon requires a bona fide effort to resolve disputes before filing a small claims case, typically through a demand letter.
Many counties in Oregon mandate mediation before proceeding to trial in small claims cases.
Attorneys are generally not permitted to represent parties in small claims court without the judge's permission.
Small claims judgments in Oregon are final and cannot be appealed.
The maximum amount you can sue for in Oregon small claims court is $10,000, excluding court costs and interest.

interesting_findings: Oregon's small claims courts emphasize self-representation and informal procedures to facilitate quick resolutions.
Mandatory mediation programs in several counties aim to reduce the number of cases proceeding to trial.
The prohibition of attorney representation without court permission helps maintain the informal nature of small claims proceedings.

sources.label: Oregon Circuit Court - Small Claims Information
ORS 46.405 - Jurisdiction of small claims department
ORS 652.140 - Payment on termination of employment
